questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive, painless weakness in his right hand. He has noticed his thumb becoming 'bumpy' and occasional twitching in his arm. Examination reveals wasting of the thenar eminence, fasciculations in the right upper limb, and brisk reflexes throughout the lower limbs. There is no sensory loss. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of unilateral hand weakness, thenar wasting, fasciculations, and brisk reflexes with no sensory loss is classic for ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis). Cervical radiculopathy usually has sensory symptoms and segmental reflex loss; multifocal motor neuropathy spares reflexes and often has conduction block; myasthenia gravis shows fatigable weakness without wasting; inclusion body myositis includes objective sensory loss and is often symmetrical.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of slurred speech and difficulty swallowing. She reports inappropriate bouts of crying. Examination shows tongue fasciculations and a brisk jaw jerk. Limb tone and power are normal, but reflexes are brisk symmetrically. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bulbar-onset MND (progressive bulbar palsy) classically presents with dysarthria, dysphagia, emotional lability, tongue fasciculations, and a brisk jaw jerk. Myasthenia gravis is fatigable and lacks fasciculations; stroke-induced pseudobulbar palsy would not have tongue fasciculations; oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy involves ptosis and external ophthalmoplegia; motor neuropathy produces flaccid weakness.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of rapidly progressive weakness in all four limbs, dysphagia, and dyspnoea. Examination reveals flaccid weakness, areflexia, and no sensory loss. CSF protein is normal. Nerve conduction studies show reduced compound muscle action potentials but no conduction block. Over the next week, he develops fasciculations and extensor plantar responses. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The rapid progression, initial flaccid areflexic weakness, and normal CSF could mimic Guillain-Barré, but the appearance of fasciculations and upper motor neuron signs (extensor plantars) within a week points to rapidly progressive ALS. Guillain-Barré would typically show elevated CSF protein and denervation without UMN signs; myasthenia has fatigability; LEMS has autonomic features and reduced reflexes; polymyositis has raised CK and myopathic EMG.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of progressive stiffness and weakness in both legs. She has difficulty walking and occasional urinary urgency. Examination reveals spastic paraparesis with brisk knee jerks and extensor plantars. Upper limbs are normal. There are no fasciculations, no sensory loss, and no cerebellar signs. MRI spine and brain are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Primary lateral sclerosis is a pure upper motor neuron variant of MND, typically presenting with symmetric spastic paraparesis, preserved power initially, and no fasciculations or sensory loss. Multiple sclerosis would have remissions, MRI lesions, and often sensory/cerebellar signs; hereditary spastic paraplegia is slowly progressive but often with family history; cervical myelopathy would show MRI changes; transverse myelitis is acute and may have sensory level.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of progressive dysphagia, weight loss, and nasal regurgitation. She has mild slurred speech. Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy is normal. Examination reveals a weak, fasciculating tongue and a brisk jaw jerk. Limb examination is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of progressive dysphagia with bulbar signs (tongue fasciculations, brisk jaw jerk) and no limb involvement is classic for bulbar-onset MND. Oesophageal carcinoma would show a structural lesion on endoscopy; myasthenia gravis is fatigable and lacks fasciculations; stroke pseudobulbar palsy would have emotional lability but no fasciculations; motor neuropathy would cause flaccid weakness.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with progressive weakness and wasting of his right hand over the past 6 months. He has noticed fasciculations in his arms and legs. Neurological examination shows reduced muscle bulk, weakness in a distal pattern, and brisk reflexes. Sensation is normal. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the cervical spine
questions.explanation: EMG is the key diagnostic test for motor neurone disease, showing features of acute and chronic denervation (fibrillations, fasciculations, large motor units). Nerve conduction studies are typically normal and used to exclude neuropathy. MRI may exclude structural causes but does not confirm MND.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with 8 months of progressive dysarthria and dysphagia, along with mild weakness in her arms. She has no sensory symptoms. Neurological examination reveals bulbar palsy and upper motor neuron signs in the limbs. Which diagnostic criteria are most commonly used to confirm motor neurone disease?
questions.explanation: The revised El Escorial criteria are the standard diagnostic criteria for MND, requiring evidence of upper and lower motor neuron involvement in multiple regions. McDonald criteria are for multiple sclerosis.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known motor neurone disease complains of shortness of breath on exertion and orthopnoea. He has a weak cough. Which investigation is most appropriate to detect early respiratory muscle weakness?
options: Forced vital capacity (FVC) in the erect position
questions.explanation: SNIP is a non-invasive, sensitive test for diaphragmatic weakness in MND, often detecting impairment before FVC declines. It is recommended by NICE for monitoring respiratory function. FVC is also used but SNIP may be more sensitive for early disease.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with motor neurone disease develops acute dyspnoea and cannot lie flat. She has known bulbar involvement. Which investigation best confirms diaphragmatic weakness?
questions.explanation: A drop in supine FVC of more than 20% compared to erect FVC is highly suggestive of diaphragmatic weakness, a common respiratory complication in MND. SNIP is also useful but supine FVC is the classic bedside test.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with bulbar-onset motor neurone disease is noted to have inappropriate behaviour and executive dysfunction. His family reports personality changes over the past year. Which cognitive assessment is most appropriate to evaluate for frontotemporal dementia?
questions.explanation: ACE-III includes tests of executive function and frontal lobe tasks, making it more sensitive than MMSE or MoCA for detecting frontotemporal dementia associated with MND. The Hachinski score differentiates vascular dementia.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive limb weakness, fasciculations, and slurred speech. Examination reveals upper and lower motor neuron signs in multiple regions. MRI brain and spine are normal, and nerve conduction studies are consistent with motor neuron disease. What is the most appropriate first-line disease-modifying therapy?
questions.explanation: Riluzole is the only NICE-approved disease-modifying drug for MND, shown to prolong survival by about 2-3 months. Edaravone is not licensed in the UK for MND. Nusinersen is for spinal muscular atrophy. Baclofen treats spasticity only, and rituximab has no role in sporadic MND.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with known motor neuron disease reports troublesome drooling that soaks her clothing and affects her social interactions. She has mild bulbar weakness. What is the first-line pharmacological treatment for sialorrhoea in MND?
options: Hyoscine hydrobromide 1–2 mg/72 hours transdermal patch
Botulinum toxin A injection into parotid glands
questions.explanation: UK guidelines recommend an anticholinergic agent first-line, typically hyoscine hydrobromide patches (or glycopyrrolonium bromide). Botulinum toxin is second-line due to invasiveness. Amitriptyline can be used but is often less effective than hyoscine. Atropine is rarely used now, and radiotherapy is reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with advanced motor neuron disease (bulbar-onset) presents to the emergency department with acute confusion and dyspnoea. Arterial blood gas shows pH 7.25, pCO2 8.5 kPa, pO2 9.0 kPa on room air. He has weak cough and pooling of secretions. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) via bi-level positive airway pressure
questions.explanation: NIV is first-line for hypercapnic respiratory failure in MND, improving survival and quality of life. Invasive ventilation is usually reserved if NIV fails or patient wishes, but not first-line. High-flow oxygen without ventilatory support may worsen hypercapnia. CPAP does not assist ventilation. Doxapram is not recommended.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with motor neuron disease has painful cramps and stiffness in his legs that interfere with sleep and mobility. He has no significant liver or kidney disease. What is the first-line medication for spasticity in MND?
questions.explanation: Baclofen is the preferred first-line antispasticity agent in MND according to NICE. Tizanidine and dantrolene are second-line options. Diazepam may be used for acute spasm but not first-line due to sedation and tolerance. Gabapentin is used for neuropathic pain, not specifically spasticity.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with motor neuron disease (progressive muscle weakness and fasciculations) requires an emergency laparotomy for a perforated duodenal ulcer. He has no known drug allergies. Which of the following neuromuscular blocking agents is CONTRAINDICATED in this patient?
questions.explanation: Suxamethonium is contraindicated in MND because denervated muscles upregulate acetylcholine receptors, leading to massive potassium release and risk of cardiac arrest. Non-depolarising agents (rocuronium, atracurium, vecuronium, cisatracurium) are safe but may require dose adjustment.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive left arm weakness, fasciculations, and mild dysarthria. Examination shows wasting and fasciculations in the left arm, hyperreflexia in both legs, and upgoing plantars. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform nerve conduction studies (NCS) and EMG
Arrange MRI brain and cervical spine
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend urgent neurology referral if MND is suspected, as diagnosis is clinical and requires specialist confirmation. MRI and EMG are important but are arranged after referral; starting riluzole requires a confirmed diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known limb-onset MND reports troublesome drooling that soaks her clothes despite good mouth care. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for botulinum toxin injections into salivary glands
questions.explanation: NICE recommends hyoscine hydrobromide transdermal patch or glycopyrrolate as first-line pharmacological treatment for sialorrhea in MND. Hyoscine patch is often preferred initially. Botulinum toxin is second-line; suction is adjunctive; amitriptyline is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with bulbar-onset MND presents to ED with acute type 2 respiratory failure (pH 7.25, pCO2 10 kPa). He is not on any respiratory support, has a cough assist device at home, and is drowsy but rousable. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends NIV as first-line treatment for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure in MND. Intubation is often avoided due to poor prognosis; high-flow oxygen is insufficient; doxapram is not recommended; palliative care is important but not the immediate action.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with MND is admitted with fever (39°C), tachycardia (110 bpm), blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, productive cough, and consolidation on chest X-ray. He has known swallowing difficulties and uses a nasogastric tube for feeds. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion
questions.explanation: He likely has aspiration pneumonia with sepsis. NICE recommends starting empiric antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav) immediately to cover oral anaerobes; blood cultures should be taken but must not delay antibiotics. The other options are not the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with MND on riluzole 50 mg twice daily for 6 months. Routine blood tests show ALT 150 IU/L (normal <40). She is asymptomatic. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Continue riluzole and repeat LFTs in 2 weeks
Reduce riluzole dose to 50 mg once daily
questions.explanation: NICE states that riluzole should be stopped if ALT rises >3 times the upper limit of normal (i.e., >120 IU/L). Dose reduction is not recommended; checking bilirubin/INR is sensible but not the immediate action; the elevation is significant enough to require discontinuation.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man presents with a 4-month history of progressive slurred speech and occasional choking when drinking. He has noticed twitching in his arms. On examination, there are fasciculations in the tongue and intrinsic hand muscles. Which finding is most concerning for a life-threatening complication of this condition?
questions.explanation: Orthopnoea suggests diaphragmatic weakness, an early sign of respiratory muscle involvement in MND. Respiratory failure is the most common cause of death, so this finding requires urgent assessment. Other findings are typical but not immediately dangerous.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known bulbar-onset motor neurone disease (MND) reports difficulty swallowing her own saliva. Over the past month she has had two chest infections requiring antibiotics. Which finding on examination is most indicative of an unsafe swallow and high risk of aspiration?
options: Weight loss of 3 kg in 6 months
questions.explanation: Coughing immediately after swallowing indicates reduced laryngeal protection and aspiration risk. Recurrent pneumonia supports this. Weight loss is important but not an immediate red flag for aspiration; a brisk gag reflex does not correlate with safe swallowing.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with advanced motor neurone disease (MND) is brought to the emergency department with acute breathlessness. His respiratory rate is 28/min, oxygen saturations 88% on room air. On examination, you note inward movement of the abdomen during inspiration. What does this sign indicate?
questions.explanation: Paradoxical (inward) abdominal movement on inspiration is a sign of diaphragmatic paralysis, a common and dangerous complication of MND leading to rapid respiratory failure. Other options are unlikely given the known diagnosis and examination.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with a 2-year history of motor neurone disease (MND) presents with acute onset of slurred speech and drooping of the right side of his mouth that began 1 hour ago. He has no limb weakness. Which finding on examination suggests this is not progression of his MND?
options: Asymmetrical lower facial weakness with forehead sparing
questions.explanation: MND typically causes bilateral, symmetrical bulbar signs. An acute, unilateral lower facial weakness with forehead sparing is typical of an upper motor neuron facial palsy from stroke, not MND. This is a red flag for a new, treatable condition.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with motor neurone disease (MND) attends clinic for a routine review. She reports morning headaches and daytime somnolence but denies dyspnoea. Spirometry shows forced vital capacity (FVC) of 48% predicted. Which finding from further assessment would confirm the need for non-invasive ventilation (NIV)?
questions.explanation: Daytime hypercapnia (PaCO2 >6.0 kPa) is a key indication for NIV in MND, especially with symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation (morning headache, somnolence). PaO2 may be normal early; peripheral oedema is a late sign of cor pulmonale.
